command: bash -c 'h1=$(md5sum /bootflash/active.img | awk "{print $1}"); h2=$(awk "{print $1}" /mnt/golden/active.img.md5); [ "$h1" = "$h2" ] || echo "ALERT: active.img MD5 mismatch"'
explanation: Computes the MD5 of the active image and compares it to a golden value to detect divergence indicating system image modification.
rationale_steps: Compute MD5 of the current active image
Compare values and raise an alert on mismatch

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Properly quoted bash -c string with balanced quotes, valid pipelines, variable assignments, and a correct test expression.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It correctly compares the MD5 of the active image to a golden hash to detect image modification (aligned with T1601 detection), though use of MD5 and assumed read permissions on network devices are minor realism gaps.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is mostly well-formed, but the awk field separator quoting inside the outer single-quoted bash -c string uses double quotes which is fine, however the awk print $1 references may not work correctly as written because $1 inside double-quoted awk strings within a bash -c single-quoted context could be interpreted inconsistently depending on shell escaping; the logic structure and brackets are otherwise balanced.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command correctly implements a defensive integrity check aligned with T1601 detection by computing the MD5 of the active firmware image and comparing it against a stored golden reference value, alerting on any mismatch that would indicate unauthorized system image modification.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with correctly balanced single and double quotes, proper command substitution syntax, and valid shell logical operators.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command effectively achieves the intent of detecting system image modification by comparing checksums, though it contains a minor shell-scripting flaw where '$1' inside double quotes within a bash -c string will be expanded by the shell rather than awk (resulting in the full md5sum output being compared rather than just the hash).
